coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of Nb-doped strontium cobaltite@GQD electrodes for high performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Kharangarh, Poonam R.
Ravindra, Nuggehalli M.
Singh, Gurmeet
Umapathy, Siva
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the fabrication and characterization of Nb-doped strontium cobaltite, SrCo0.9Nb0.1O3-δ (SCN), with a significant concentration of oxygen vacancies, as a charge storage material, prepared using sol-gel method, is reported. The results obtained show that the diffusion of oxygen ions is a rate limiting factor for charge storage. The perovskite material, niobium doped strontium cobalitite, on nano graphene sheets such as SCN@GQDs, has been prepared. The Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) have been synthesized by using a facile hydrothermal method. The morphology of SCN@GQDs was characterized by FESEM, and TEM. Additional characterization techniques such as PXRD, Raman Spectroscopy, FTIR, TGA/DTA, and BET were performed to observe the structure, porosity, functional groups, defects and thermal behavior of the material. The prepared SCN@GQDs act as an effective electrode material and exhibit high energy density. These SCN@GQD electrodes have been investigated for their electro-chemical properties. The specific capacitance of the synthesized perovskite materials, at 0.1 Ag−1 for supercapacitor, is higher than those of GQDs and SCN due to the improved conductivity, presence of oxygen vacancies and fast ion diffusion between the working electrodes and electrolyte. These properties of SCN@GQD perovskite material, with nanographene sheets, for boosting the capacity of electrodes, provide excellent opportunities for applications in supercapacitors. This perovskite material is superior in comparison to other candidates due to its larger oxygen vacancy concentration and higher oxygen-ion mobility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Hydrothermal method
Nb-doped strontium cobaltite (SCN)
Supercapacitor electrode
K4FeCN6 electrolyte

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Required materials for synthesis of perovskites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM-EDX (Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy with Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy) images of GQDs (a), SCN (b), & SCN@GQDs (c) and (c) EDX images(d–f) after adsorption FESEM elemental mapping image and their selective element mapping images of C,O (g) Al, Sr,Nb,Co (h) and C,O,Sr, Nb, Co(i) elements for GQDs, SCN, & SCN@GQDs.
(a–c): shows TEM images of GQDs, SCN and SCN@GQDs. Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) of final product SCN@GQDs has been shown in inset of panel (c).
(a) Perovskite structure of SCN@GQDs; (b) XRD of GO, GQDs, SCN & SCN@GQDs; (c) Thermogravimetric analysis in nitrogen atmosphere; and (d) FT-IR spectrum of GO, GQDs, SCN & SCN@GQDs.
(a) Raman spectra of GO, GQDs, SCN& SCN@GQDs; and (b) BET of GQDs, SCN& SCN@GQDs.
Electrochemical measurements (i–iii) Cyclic voltammetry measurements of GQDs, SCN, SCN@GQDs at different scan rates and; (iv) impedance measurements of GQDs, SCN & SCN@GQDs electrodes.

(a) GCD characteristics of SCN, SCN@GQDs at current density of 0.1 A/g (b) Cycling stability of GQDs, SCN, SCN @GQDs; and (c) The b value determination of normalized anodic and cathodic peak currents of SCN@GQDs.
The charge storage mechanism of the SCN@GQD electrode.
Preparation scheme of hydrothermally prepared GQDs; SCN by using sol gel and SCN@GQDs via hydrothermal method.
BET results of GQDs, SCN and SCN@GQDs.
A characteristic table of GQDs, SrCo0.9Nb0.1O3-δ (SCN), SrCo0.9Nb0.1O3-δ (SCN) @GQDs perovskite materials.
A comparison table on the fabricated GQDs-based supercapacitor devices.

row.entry: 1750, 1573.65 and 1434.37 in LiCl, NaCl and KCl aq. electrolytes,

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of Nb-doped strontium cobaltite@GQD electrodes for high performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and AstroPhysics, University of Delhi, Delhi 110007, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and AstroPhysics, University of Delhi, Delhi 110007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Delhi, Delhi 110007, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Delhi, Delhi 110007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic and Physical Chemistry, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore 560012, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Inorganic and Physical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Inorganic and Physical Chemistry, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore 560012, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, New Jersey Institute of Technology, Newark, NJ 07102, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, New Jersey Institute of Technology, Newark, New Jersey 07102, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics and AstroPhysics, University of Delhi, Delhi 110007, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the fabrication and characterization of Nb-doped strontium cobaltite, SrCo0.9Nb0.1O3-δ (SCN), with a significant concentration of oxygen vacancies, as a charge storage material, prepared using sol-gel method, is reported. The results obtained show that the diffusion of oxygen ions is a rate limiting factor for charge storage. The perovskite material, niobium doped strontium cobalitite, on nano graphene sheets such as SCN@GQDs, has been prepared. The Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) have been synthesized by using a facile hydrothermal method. The morphology of SCN@GQDs was characterized by FESEM, and TEM. Additional characterization techniques such as PXRD, Raman Spectroscopy, FTIR, TGA/DTA, and BET were performed to observe the structure, porosity, functional groups, defects and thermal behavior of the material. The prepared SCN@GQDs act as an effective electrode material and exhibit high energy density. These SCN@GQD electrodes have been investigated for their electro-chemical properties. The specific capacitance of the synthesized perovskite materials, at 0.1 Ag−1 for supercapacitor, is higher than those of GQDs and SCN due to the improved conductivity, presence of oxygen vacancies and fast ion diffusion between the working electrodes and electrolyte. These properties of SCN@GQD perovskite material, with nanographene sheets, for boosting the capacity of electrodes, provide excellent opportunities for applications in supercapacitors. This perovskite material is superior in comparison to other candidates due to its larger oxygen vacancy concentration and higher oxygen-ion mobility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, energy storage devices are essential due to the increase in the use of renewable sources of energy and the resulting demands on the grid infrastructure. In order to address these critical issues, cost-effective and environmentally friendly materials for energy storage devices, with high energy density, long life cycle, long shelf life, and fast charge-discharge, are required. The most commonly utilized energy storage devices include batteries, supercapacitors (SCs), and hybrid devices [1–6]. So far, SCs have been reported to exhibit low energy density and low capacitance values which limits their use in a wide range of emerging applications [7,8]. In order to overcome these limitations and increase the capacity of SCs, suitable materials need to be developed. A group of carbon based materials and transition metal oxides/hydroxides represents a potential option. Carbon based materials demonstrate electrochemical double layer capacitance (EDLC) behavior and transition metal oxides/hydroxides exhibit charge accumulation through pseudocapacitance (PC). Recently, research on perovskite materials has become very attractive due to the resulting increase in energy density and life expectancy caused by higher operating potential window.

The main drawback of SCs can be attributed to its low surface area. Higher capacity in SCs can be accomplished by utilizing ion intercalation mechanism in bulk electrode for energy storage. Maximum intercalation-type SC electrodes have been reported in the literature in which positive ions act as the charge carriers [9,10]; anions are not of concern due to their large ion size. The perovskites materials, SrCoO3−δ (SCO) [11], Sr6Co5O15 [12], and SrCoO2.5 [13], with hexagonal or orthorhombic structure, are excellent candidates as cathode material for high performance SCs. These SCs can be utilized in Intermediate-Temperature (IT)-Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC) applications [14,15]. Besides, SCN is limited by the thermal stability of its crystal structure; it exhibits transformation into a 3C-type cubic structure (T > 950 °C) which might not be structurally favorable with tolerance factor (t = 1.04) higher than the ideal value (t = 1). In order to reduce the tolerance factor, the main approach is to stabilize the 3C-type perovskite structure by adding Sr2+ to smaller cations such as La3+, Ce3+, and Y3+, or a partial substitution of Co3+/4+ with larger cations, such as Sb5+, Nb5+, Mo6+, and Ta5+ [16–21]. The approach of doping with higher oxidation-state cations will lead to stability in SCO based materials by compensating for O2 loss at higher temperatures. Nb2O5 has been considered to be a dopant for the SCO system; it stabilizes the active cubic structure for SOFCs.

The various nanoparticles such as SnO2 from the thermal decomposition [22], Cerium Oxide (CeO2) via hydrothermal route [23] have been reported. The fabricated nanocomposites and flexible supercapacitors in terms of symmetric (SC), asymmetric (ASC), solid state were used in various applications such as 3D hierarchical flower-shaped AlV3O9/SnO2 nanocomposites as a cathode electrode in Lithium Ion Batteries (LIB) [24]; asymmetric flexible solid-state supercapacitors based on Zn0.76Co0.24S-NC as positive electrode and bimetallic MOF derived nitrogen-doped mesoporous carbon (MPC) as negative electrode (ZCS//MPC-ASC) [25]; flexible solid state supercapacitor Cu7S4-NWs coated on carbon fiber fabric (CFF) or (Cu7S4-NWs-CFF) [26]; flexible asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) devices using Zn–Co–O@CC as a positive electrode and bimetallic organic framework (MOF)-derived nanoporous carbon polyhedra (NPC@CC) as a negative electrode (Zn–Co–O@CC//NPC@CC) for ASC devices [27]. Molybdenum sulfifide (MoS2) hierarchical nanospheres on carbon cloth (CC) [28], vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) nanosheets grown directly on titanium (Ti) substrate (f-V2O5-Ti//Zn) [29], AlV3O9/MWCNTs (AV-CNT) [30], AlV3O9/Graphene (AV-G) and AlV3O9/GQDs (AV-Q), Co3O4/ZnO for removal of Methylene Blue (MB) and Rhodamine RB dyes from water [31], FeVO4/V2O5 for photocatalytic ability [32], NiAl2O4/NiO using thermal decomposition for the electrochemical hydrogen storage [33] have been developed.

In 2008, the single-phase SrNb0.1Co0.9O3-δ (SNC) was considered to be a potential cathode, with low polarization resistance (temp. < 600 °C); it exhibited an improvement in the performance of fuel cells (~40 %) [34]. In 2010, Shao et al. [35] prepared SrCo0.9Nb0.1O3-δ (SCN) perovskite oxide by utilizing high-energy ball milling method and monitoring by calcination at 1100 °C. Order-disorder phase transition was avoided by doping with highly charged Nb5+ ions which helps to stabilize the perovskite structure and simplify the formation of Co2+ ions in the perovskite lattice; this increases the oxygen non-stoichiometry. The obtained low thermal coefficient of expansion in SCN perovskite oxide will be of interest for applications in LTSOFC.

In 2016; Z. Shao et al. [36] demonstrated a perovskite type SrCo0.9Nb0.1O3-δ (SCN) as a novel anion-intercalated electrode material for SCs; this electrode material was coupled with activated carbon i.e. SCN/AC for the formation of asymmetric supercapacitor with a very high volumetric capacitance of an extraordinary cycling stability, charge retention rate and high energy density. Also, in 2016, Sossina M. Haile [37] studied the bulk properties of SrCo0.9Nb 0.1O3-δ for O2 reduction catalyst electrochemically. They found that this material has more thermodynamic stability, can be used as an air electrode for SOFC and has large chemical expansivity. In 2017, K. Huang et al. [38] synthesized SrCo0.9Nb0.1O2.72 (SCN10) by traditional solid-state reactions which was measured by neutron diffraction with variation in temperature (VTND) and thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) revealing a tetragonal (P4/mmm) structure at a temperature lower than 75 °C and a phase change from tetragonal (P4/mmm) to cubic at 412 °C (Pm3m).

Based on the suitability of SrCo0.9Nb0.1O3-δ (SCN) perovskite material as a candidate for applications in energy storage device, this work presents a study on its fabrication and characterization. Vacancies of O2 are formed in the SCN perovskite material at elevated temperatures. The SCN coupled with Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), SCN@GQDs, are active electrodes for application in SCs. The SCN@GQDs deliver an excellent charge capacity, high energy density, and long life cycle as a superior electrode material for applications in SCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Required materials for synthesis of perovskites
ce:section.ce:para: Graphite powder (Alfa, Aesar, purity-98.5 %), NaNO3 (purity ~99 %), KMnO4, (purity~99 %), KOH (purity ~ 99.99 %), 30 % H2O2 and HCl (purity ~ 98 %), Niobium(V) oxide, Nb2O5(purity >99.5 %), Co (NO3)2·6H2O (purity >98 %), Sr (NO3)2·6H2O (purity ~ 99 %) and Urea (purity >98 %) were acquired from Alfa Aesar. DI water (ρ = 18.2 MΩcm) was used in all the experiments during cleansing.

The complete synthesis has been described in a schematic diagram (Scheme 1 ) which combines the following three steps. The first step includes the preparation of graphene oxide (GO) synthesized by modified Hummers and Offemann method [39]. GQDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method for cutting the graphene sheets; this process has been described earlier in a similar work [40–42]. In the second stage, the nano-particles of SrCo0.9Nb0.1 were synthesized by sol-gel method [43]. In order to fabricate SrCo0.9Nb0.1, three different powders of 5.5 g of H8N2O10Sr, 7 g of Co (NO3)2.6H2O and 0.7 g Nb2O5 were mixed in 50 mL aqueous solution of urea (9.8 g CH₄N₂O in 50 mL DI water) by maintaining a ratio of (1:1) to form spheres. The subsequent mixture was then stirred for another half hour at 100 °C to prepare a perfect gel using a magnetic stirrer. A proper viscous gel was obtained by stirring for an additional three hours to get the final product. The obtained gel was calcined for ten hours at 1050 °C for removing the organic impurities. The resulting sample, SrCo0.9Nb0.1, was cooled at ambient temperature (RT). For the third stage, a suitable material was prepared by using SrCo0.9Nb0.1 perovskite material with GQD fragments for supercapacitor applications. In brief, SrCo0.9Nb0.1 (100 mg) and GQDs (50 mg) were mixed in 50 mL of DI water and transferred into a Teflon-autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The formed sample of SCN@GQDs was washed with DI water, acetone, hot water and ethanol several times and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

For the preparation of the electrode, 10 mg SCN@GQDs sample and 10 wt% Acetylene black (15 wt%) were dissolved in a 10 mL beaker with nafion (15 μL) using isopropyl alcohol (IPA) as dispersing medium. The solution was put in a sonicator for 20 min. The attained suspensions were homogeneously coated on 0.77 cm2 area glassy electrode and dried in open air for 6 h.

The samples have been characterized by utilizing the following techniques: Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) with EDX spectra with elemental mapping, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and Thermogravimetry (TGA, LINSEIS STA PT1000). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area measurements and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution (Micromeritics ASAP 2020, USA) were recorded by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms at −200 °C after degassing at 250 °C for 10 h. Electro-chemical performance testing was performed by using an electro-chemical work station (CHI760D) with 3-electrode system in a 0.01 M K4FeCN6 aqueous electrolyte at room temperature (RT). An analogous procedure was followed for the characterization of working electrodes [44].

Fig. 1 (a–c) and (d–f) shows FESEM images and EDS spectra with X-ray mapping of GQDs, SCN and SCN@GQDs, respectively. From Fig. 1(a), the obtained size of GQDs was determined to be in the range of 20–50 nm. Fig. 1(b) reveals the tetragonal phase with dimensions of 80–100 nm for SCN perovskite material. The reduction in size was observed in SCN@GQDs composite material with 10–30 nm and is shown in Fig. 1(c). The perovskite material with GQDs, such as SCN@GQDs composite, was found to be more crystalline in nature in comparison to GQDs and SCN. This is due to the introduction of nanoparticles of graphene which reduces the cluster formation in SCN due to its highly conducting and binding nature. Another possibility is due to the larger ionic radius of Sr2+ (1.26 Å), in comparison to that of Co2+ (0.65 Å), resulting from Co element substituted with Sr. EDX spectra with X-Ray mapping, shown in Fig. (g–i), confirm the presence of Sr, Co, Nb, and O elements. The electrostatic, Van der Waal and dipole-dipole interactions occur between graphene nanosheets and Sr(II/III), Nb(V), Co(III/IV) cations during the hydrothermal process; this is because of the existence of O2 related functional group on the surface. These results confirm that the morphology of SCN is improved by the addition of GQDs, culminating in improving the performance of the fabricated SCN@GQD composites electrochemically.

Fig. 2 (a–c) shows TEM images of GQDs, SCN and SCN@GQDs. Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) of final product SCN@GQDs has been shown in inset of Fig. 1(c). From Fig. 1(a), the obtained size of GQDs was in the range of 10–50 nm. The nanometer scale was found due to cutting of larger size (μm) graphene sheet in nanometer scale via hydrothermal process. Fig. 1(b) reveals the tetragonal phase with size 50–200 nm for SCN perovskite material. The reduction in size was observed in SCN@GQDs composite material with 10–30 nm which is shown in Fig. 1(c). The perovskite material with GQDs such as SCN@GQDs composite was found to be more crystalline in nature in comparison to GQDs and SCN. The introduction of nanoparticles of graphene reduces the clusters which are formed in SCN due to their highly conducting and binding nature.

Fig. 3(a) shows the perovskite structure of SC and doping with Nb to form SCN. XRD pattern of GO, GQDs, SCN and SCN@GQDs, with well-defined characteristic peaks, are shown in Fig. 3 (b). The average crystallite size of SCN and SCN@GQDs is calculated to be 13.06 nm and 22.48 nm, respectively, by using the well-known Scherrer formula [5]; this is in agreement with the tetragonal phase along with P4/mmm space group [38]. In comparison, all the observed diffraction peaks of GQDs were found to be identical with the hexagonal phase; this has been explained in earlier studies [40–42].

Fig. 3 (c) shows the results of the thermogravimetric analysis of the as-prepared samples of GO, GQDs, SCN and SCN@GQDs. These measurements were performed in nitrogen and oxygen atmosphere, in the room temperature (RT) to 800 °C range, to investigate their thermal behavior and oxygen vacancy concentration. The weight loss from the TGA graph was calculated for GO (~20 % and 25 %) at ~100 °C; in the case of GQDs, the weight loss (~32 % and 13 %) was estimated in the temperature range of 150–300 °C. The calculated weight loss in SCN (~20 %) and SCN@GQDs (~10 %) were obtained at 400 °C. The choice of temperatures was based on the (i) evaporation of trapped water molecules (>100 °C) and (ii) thermal decomposition due to the presence of O2 vacancies and burning of graphene into nanoparticles at higher temperatures. The concentration of oxygen vacancies was determined by the difference in weight loss between air and nitrogen atmosphere based on the report by other investigators [45,46]. The concentrations of O2 vacancy in SCN and SCN@GQDs samples were calculated to be 16.36 and 7.7 at. %, at 400 °C, respectively.

Fig. 3(d) shows the FTIR spectra of GO, GQDs, SCN and SCN@GQDs in the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm −1. The FTIR spectra of pristine GO, GQDs were briefly explained in our previous work [40,42]. In SCN, the observed six bands were manifested at 463, 563, 670, 864, 1454, and 2106 cm−1. The presence of two diffuse absorption bands in the range of 400–600 cm−1 and 610–1200 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration bands of NbO (~563 cm−1), CoO and SrO which is confirmed with references [47–49] in the literature. The absorption band in the range of 548–570 cm−1 confirms the stretching vibrations of Co(III)O bond in the CoO6 octahedron [50], whereas the stretching vibration in the region of 653–663 cm−1 is responsible for Co(II)O bond in tetrahedron CoO4; these results are consistent with the literature [47]. The weak absorption peaks, observed in the region of 500–600 cm−1, is due to bridging Nb-O-Nb bonds and those in the range 450–550 cm−1 are mainly due to the vibration of the metal cation (Sr2+). Also, in SCN@GQDs, absorption bands were observed at 563, 654, 854, 1487, and 2085 cm−1. A slight shift in the absorption bands has been observed due to the presence of normal modes of vibration of planar XO3 molecules/ion (where X = carbon content). The presence of two strong bands at 670 and 567 cm−1 confirm that the spinel structure of Co3O4 [51] has been formed. The appearance of a band at 400–600 cm−1 establishes the formation of perovskite oxide due to bonding of SrCO3 with Co3O4 and Nb oxide structure. It also reveals that SrCO3 bonds with CoO6 and the corresponding absorption bands are in the region of 400–500 cm−1, resulting in the formation of SCN which is consistent with XRD analysis (Fig. 3 (b)). Also, C-C-related peaks, in the region of 1487 cm−1, confirm the linking of the GQDs on SCN. The vibrational band near ~2085 cm−1 is due to the presence of OH stretching vibration and deformation of water molecule adsorption [41].

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool to explore the atomic structure of the obtained samples: GO, GQDs, SCN, and SCN@GQDs; these spectra are shown in Fig. 4 (a). The observed D-bands and G-bands in samples GO and GQDs are at 1359 cm−1 and 1574 & 1602 cm−1, respectively. The observed shift in G band is due to the presence of defects. The disordered nature and defects in the structure of graphitic carbon ratio were found through the peak intensities of the “D and G bands” (ID/IG). The observed ID/IG for GO, GQDs, and SCN@GQDs were 0.93, 1.01, and 1.03 respectively. The higher ID/IG of SCN@GQDs reveals disordered structure due to the intercalation of doped atoms into the graphene nano-sheets. It suggests that the intercalation of strontium, cobalt and niobium atoms and O2 vacancy related defects into the conjugated carbon has led to more defects on the surface of GQDs, improving the physical and chemical properties. The active Raman modes were observed at ~137, ~289, ~443, ~518, ~608, ~683, ~810 cm−1 in SCN; while in SCN@GQDs, the observed modes were at ~323, ~420, ~494, ~523, ~621, ~694 cm−1. The expected phonon distribution can be explained by Group Theory having six modes which include one IR active mode (i.e. 5T1u) and five Raman active modes (A1g; Eg; 3T2g). The determination of active modes corresponds to dipole moments of oxygen and both A and B site ions in the spinel structure [52,53]. The Eg mode is due to the symmetric bending of the O+ anion; the occurrence of symmetric stretching of the O2 anion corresponds to A1g mode and T2g mode corresponds to the asymmetric stretching of the oxygen anion to the tetrahedral and octahedral cations [54]. The observed Raman modes at low wave number (~616 cm−1) describe a shoulder-like feature. These peaks are assigned to A1g(1) and A1g(2) modes, demonstrating the stretching vibrations of the SrO and CoO bonds in tetrahedral sites. Small shifts in the samples were observed after the addition of carbon. This is a result of relocation of the atoms in SCN@GQDs samples. The rearrangement of atoms can be determined by relating the A1g(1) and A1g(2) mode intensities. These observed results confirm that, due to the presence of oxygen, Sr, Nb, Co related defects and non-uniform distribution of dopants provide a disordered structure which is consistent with XRD and FESEM results.

Fig. 4(b) reveals N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distribution measurements to explore the mesoporous features of GQDs, SCN and SCN@GQDs. From BET measurements, the specific surface area of GQDs, SCN, and SCN@GQDs, were found to be 10.12, 17.37, 24.01 m2 g−1 respectively and are shown in Table I . These results show that, amongst the samples considered in this study, SCN@GQDs has more surface area in comparison to SCN and GQDs. As is known in the literature, these perovskite materials do not exhibit high surface area; however, they show extraordinary performance in energy storage devices. A narrow size distribution has been obtained in SCN@GQDs and it leads to a structure with nanopores; SCN and GQDs exhibit wide pore distribution or mesopores suggesting a hierarchical mesoporous structure. Additionally, the diffusion of ions between SCN@GQDs and KOH aqueous electrolyte rapidly progress due to the escalation in active defects or sites and various impurities.

Using the IUPAC classification, SCN is known to be a type III isotherm relating to H3 hysteresis loop and reveals that the observed solids involve aggregates or agglomerates of particles with a non-uniform size and/or shape. The isotherm curve of SCN@GQDS relates to type IV and the hysteresis is a combination of type H2 and H4. Types H2 and H4 typically indicate the mesoporosity with small amount of microporosity. These factors help to make these perovskites as excellent electrode material candidates for energy storage applications [55].

In order to determine the possibility of utilizing GQDs, SCN and SCN@GQDs as electrodes in energy storage devices, CV measurements were performed in K4FeCN6 electrolyte with different scan rates from 5 mVs−1 to 50 mVs−1, at an interval of ~10 mVs−1 (Fig. 5 (i–iii)). From the CV measurements, redox peaks were observed, which suggest the pseudo-capacitor behavior in GQDs, SCN and SCN@GQD electrodes. The first peak (anodic) in CV of SCN appears due to the oxidation of Co2+ to Co3+ exhibiting the filling of O2 vacancies through the intercalation of O2 ions. The other peak (cathodic) provides an assessment of the oxidation of Co3+ to Co4+ and reveals the intercalation of excess oxygen ions in SCN structure. These results suggest that when the cobalt ions are oxidized, H2O gets separated due to the simultaneous process of O2 ions which are intercalated into vacancy and could transfer protons to other OH ions. The specific capacitance of the prepared electrode is found to be higher at 5 mVs−1 in the K4FeCN6 aqueous electrolyte, suggesting the appearance of OH groups due to Intercalation-Type Energy Storage (ITES). A similar result (two pairs of redox peaks) is observed in SCN@GQDs with higher specific capacitance as function of decrease in scan rate. Additionally, the peak current in SCN@GQDS electrodes increases with scan rate, due to the rapid electronic and ionic-transport rates [56]. However, the nanosize of graphene layer or GQDs and the associated edge effect as well as its highly conductive nature enhances the diffusion of ions, resulting in transport of electrons via active sites. As a result, the higher SCs of SCN@GQDs can be attributed to vacancies related to oxygen and structural stability. This characteristic can be utilized for fabricating efficient electrodes in energy storage applications.

Fig. 5(iv) demonstrates the Nyquist plot of SCN, SCN@GQDs; the corresponding equivalent circuit has been shown in inset of Fig. 5 (iv); the calculated values of internal resistance (Ri) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the GQDs, SCN, SCN@GQDs are shown in Table II . Actually, the SCN@GQD electrode leads to lower Ri and Rct as compared to SCN and GQD (kΩ) electrodes due to increased conductivity of electrons in fragments of graphene. These results are consistent with those in the published literature [13,16]. The presence of defects in these materials results in an increase in conductivity. In addition, the active tetragonal structure and the vacancies created in SCN@GQDs lead to enhanced diffusion of ions in aqueous electrolyte for the synthesized oxide materials.

The Galvanostatic Charge–Discharge (GCD) curves of the SCN and SCN@GQD electrodes are demonstrated with current density in Fig. 6(a). The calculated specific capacitance of SCN@GQDs was 485.8 Fg−1 at current density of 0.1 Ag−1. The specific capacitance of SCN@GQDs was found to be higher in comparison to those of SCN and GQDs. The results obtained are shown in Table II. From GCD curves, there is no two phase electrode reaction during oxygen intercalation or removal process; this leads to higher specific capacitance by providing a strong support via SCN bulk phase for energy storage applications [57]. The observed higher specific capacitance, based on GCD curves from lower current density to higher current density, indicates that the fast reaction between surface and bulk diffusion confirms lower charge resistance with higher diffusion rate of oxygen [15].

In order to produce a good electrode for energy storage applications, the cycling stability of the electrode is a critical issue. This is due to the variation in energy storage device performance. In order to verify the cycling stability of SCN@GQDs, the authors tested the electrodes with a few number of cycles up to 490 which is shown in Fig. 6(b). From Fig. 6(b), it can be observed that the specific capacitance initially improved in the first 50 cycles, followed by slight decrease in successive cycles up to 490 with 61 % retention rate. Initially, the capacitance increases up to 50 cycles due to slow activation of electrode and electrode/electrolyte interface, which depicts the improved action of SCN@GQDs electrodes during the activation process. It is assumed that the surface area of working electrodes must be increased due to the slow activity of the cations that are present during the activation process. This suggests that the generation of oxygen vacancies increased, which begin to act as active sites to maintain charge tolerance along with the cation activity. As a result, active sites of electrodes become more approachable by natural process due to modified contacts through the electrode-electrolyte interface. However, Cs of 485.8 Fg−1 has been retained even after 490 cycles, showing excellent cycling stability; this is consistent with the results in the literature [58–62]. Up to 490 cycles, specific capacitance becomes almost constant revealing the high stability of the perovskite structure. A comparison table (Table III ) of all the various reported electrode materials, doped with GQDs, has been presented with their calculated specific capacitance at a particular current density.

Fig. 6(c) shows the logarithm plot of peak current (ip) versus scan rate; it depends on power law and follows the equation; i = avb [63–65]. The power law has been applied to define the process of charge storage. The value of b can be determined from the slope in Fig. 6(c) and it varies from 0 to 1. However, the value of b is not clear in the literature [65]. Ideally, the value of b equal to 1(b = 1) suggests the surface diffusion process, while the value of b = 0.5 reveals linear diffusion. However, with low electrode conductivity, the rate performance is electrically-limited. As conductivity is increased by using GQDs in SCN perovskites material, the electrical limitations are reduced; this shifts the rate-limiting factor towards more diffusion. This is consistent with the fact that the rate-limitation is associated with electron transport. Moreover, ion mobility is comparable to the ionic conductivity. As a result, conductivity increases due to increase in mobile ions. From Fig. 6(c), both cathodic and anodic peaks are <1 at region 1 (n < 15 mVs−1), demonstrating the capacitive behavior. On the other hand, at region 2, both peaks have the value of b which is also <1; this suggests that oxygen ion diffusion is the rate limiting process. We assume that the particles of cobalt ions are stacked up on the surface of glassy electrode and the pores of graphene nanoparticles in SCN layer fill up the electrolyte. Therefore, this leads to enhanced diffusion of O2- ions throughout the complete layer, and will directly react with the electrolyte in the pore. This may be the reason for the value of b to be smaller than 1 in the present work as compared to published literature [63–65].

The charge storage mechanism of the SCN@GQD electrode is shown in Fig. 7 with possible associated reactions. The mechanisms during oxidation process (Co2+/Co3+) are as follows: (i) filling of O2 vacancies through intercalation of an electrolyte; (ii) oxygen ion via OH [36]; (iii) diffusion of O2 along the edges of unit cell and oxidation process (Co3+/Co4+) is possibly due to intercalation of excess O2 on the surface by the diffusion of Co3+. In general, the surface reaction is difficult when O2 ions are incorporated into oxygen vacancies from -OH groups.

Recently, a significant amount of work on perovskite materials, doped with different transition metals, has been reported in the literature. Nb is considered to be an important transition metal with a high melting point, high boiling point, and high dielectric constant on metal surfaces and forms many type of compounds. Furthermore, Nb is frequently utilized as a doping agent. Although the prepared SCN@GQD electrode exhibited small resistance, good charging and discharging effect, good stability, there are still many problems to be addressed that need to be solved in the near future. The preparation methods of SCN@GQDs, involving sol gel and hydrothermal assisted calcination methods need to be further developed. The synthesized particles can easily agglomerate during calcination resulting lower in porosity. Hence, it is difficult to employ these methods for large-scale application. Therefore, a method which is environmentally friendly and can be utilized for large-scale production needs to be developed. Optimizing the structure of perovskite materials with control in morphology (increase in active sites and O2 related defects) will lead to improvement in energy storage applications; this still needs to be widely explored. Currently, the combination of metal oxides and carbon nano-materials is of enormous interest; this combination is considered to be a good candidate for anode or cathode materials with higher conductivity and a reversible pseudo-capacitor behavior. The introduction of graphene quantum dots into SCN based oxides will lead to increase in capacity for obtaining the best electrochemical performance due to the interaction between graphene quantum dots with transition metal oxides based materials. The synthesized electrodes have some merits. The sol gel method, that was used to synthesize SCN, is inexpensive, easy to use and is environmentally friendly. Hydrothermal method was used to prepare GQDs to obtain uniformity in size. The phase transformation of SCN does not occur during O2− intercalation/deintercalation process unlike in the case of Li-ion batteries (LIB) and sodium ion batteries (SIB). SCN electrode stores charge through intercalation pseudocapacitive mechanism. As a result, carbon-based nano material such as GQDs can be used as an anode/cathode in a hybrid cell or symmetric/asymmetric flexible supercapacitor for practical applications. Due to the large diffusion coefficient of OH ions, high concentration of oxygen vacancies in the matrix, high electrical conductivity, and low resistance, the synthesized electrodes show high capacity. In future studies, hybrid supercapacitors (HSCs) will be the next generation energy storage devices bridging the performance gap between conventional batteries and supercapacitors. In general, HSC combines the use of batteries and supercapacitors. These high capacity devices will become the standard for energy storage in the industry.

In this study, sol-gel method was used to dope strontium cobaltite electrode (SCN) with Nb to explore materials for energy storage applications. SCN@GQDs electrodes were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. Due to the highly conducting nature of graphene, fragments of graphene were used in SCN resulting in SCN@GQDs electrodes. Excellent performance characteristics such as specific capacitance and good cycling stability were observed due to excessive O2 vacancies in the matrix, resulting in enhanced diffusion and higher conductivity. SCN@GQD perovskites, as electrode material for applications in energy storage applications, were synthesized and characterized. The results of this study show that the use of graphene is promising and leads to higher concentration of O2− vacancies in Nb-doped strontium cobalitite-based oxygen electrode materials. It is anticipated that these perovskite oxides based electrodes, prepared by using graphene, will be considered as significant and promising electrodes in-future supercapacitor applications.
Conception and design of study: Gurmeet Singh, Siva Umapathy, Poonam R. Kharangarh; and Nuggehalli M. Ravindra.
Acquisition of data: Poonam R. Kharangarh; Nuggehalli M. Ravindra.
Analysis and/or interpretation of data: Nuggehalli M. Ravindra; Poonam R. Kharangarh.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Department of Science & Technology, New Delhi, India, under Women Scientists Scheme-A (WOS-A), reference number (SR/WOS-A/PM-107/2017). The authors thank Prof. S. Annapoorni, Department of Physics and Astrophysics, University of Delhi, for providing us support during the pandemic. The support of USIC, Department of Chemistry, University of Delhi and AIIMS, Delhi in providing various characterization techniques is acknowledged with thanks. The authors thank Prof. R.K. Sharma, Department of Chemistry, and University of Delhi for facilitating the electrochemical measurements.
